title: Case on Immunity for Crimes against Relatives
text:
- 32 -Case on Immunity for Crimes against Relatives
[2020Hun-Ma468, 2020Hun-Ba341 (consolidated), 2021Hun-Ba420 (consolidated) , 
2024Hun-Ma146 (consolidated), June 27, 2024]
Complainants
The same as listed in Appendix
Underlying Cases 
Gwangju High Court (Jeonju) 2020ChoJae108 
Request for Review of Non-Prosecution Decision (2020Hun-Ba341) 
Seoul High Court 2021ChoJae3123 
Request for Review of Non-Prosecution Decision (2021Hun-Ba420)
Decided
June 27, 2024
Holding
Article 328, Section (1) of the Criminal Act (amended by Act No. 
7427 on March 31, 2005) does not conform to the Constitution. Courts, 
other State organs, and local governments must suspend the application 
of this provision until the legislature amends it no later than December 
31, 2025.
Reasoning
I. Overview of the Case
A. 2020Hun-Ma468
1. Complainant K.J. is an individual with a third-degree intellectual 
disability. On July 2, 2019, attorney Lee Hyunwoo was appointed as his 
“specific guardian” (Busan Family Court 2019NeDan200364).
- 33 -2. Complainant K.J. filed criminal complaints against his uncle K.H., 
aunt B.J., and cousins K.S.-1 and K.S.-2 for quasi-fraud and embezzlement. 
On December 31, 2019, a prosecutor from the Busan District 
Prosecutors’ Office issued decisions of “non-prosecution” for K.H. and 
B.J. due to absence of prosecutorial authority citing immunity based on 
their status as cohabiting relatives of Complainant, and decisions of 
“non-prosecution” for K.S.-1 and K.S.-2 due to insufficient evidence 
(Busan District Prosecutors’ Office HyeongJe24878 of 2019).
3. In response, on March 26, 2020, Complainant K.J. filed the instant 
constitutional complaint against Article 328, Section (1), Article 354, and 
Article 361 of the Criminal Act, all of which provide for immunity for 
property crimes committed against relatives.
B. 2020Hun-Ba341
1. Complainant K.C. filed a criminal complaint for embezzlement 
against his stepfather L.Y. However, on March 17, 2020, a prosecutor 
from the Jeonju District Prosecutors’ Office issued a non-prosecution 
decision due to absence of prosecutorial authority, citing immunity based 
on L.Y.’s status as a cohabiting relative of Complainant (Jeonju District 
Prosecutors’ Office HyeongJe23845 of 2019).
2. On April 9, 2020, Complainant K.C. filed a request for review of 
the non-prosecution decision (Gwangju High Court [Jeonju] 2020ChoJae 
108). While the case was pending, he petitioned the court to request 
constitutional review of Article 328, Section (1) of the Criminal Act 
(Gwangju High Court [Jeonju] 2020ChoGi6). Both the request and 
petition were denied on May 22, 2020. Complainant subsequently 
appealed the denial of the review request, but this was dismissed on 
February 5, 2021 (Supreme Court 2020Mo1879).
- 34 -3. On June 5, 2020, Complainant K.C. applied for the appointment of 
a public defender for the purpose of filing a constitutional complaint. A 
decision to appoint a public defender was rendered on June 16, 2020 
(2020Hun-Sa620), and on June 26, 2020, Complainant filed the instant 
constitutional complaint against Article 328, Section (1) of the Criminal 
Act.
C. 2021Hun-Ba420
1. Complainant J.Y., acting on behalf of her father who suffers from 
Parkinson’s disease, filed criminal complaints against her siblings J.S. 
and J.K. for occupational embezzlement. However, on August 6, 2021, a 
prosecutor from the Seoul Central District Prosecutors’ Office issued a 
non-prosecution decision due to absence of prosecutorial authority, citing 
that J.S. and J.K., as lineal blood relatives of Complainant’s father, 
qualified for immunity (Seoul Central District Prosecutors’ Office 
HyeongJe37870 of 2021).
2. On November 10, 2021, Complainant J.Y. filed a request for review 
of the non-prosecution decision (Seoul High Court 2021ChoJae3123) 
and, while the case was pending, petitioned the court to request 
constitutional review of Articles 328, 344, and 361 of the Criminal Act 
(Seoul High Court 2021ChoGi429). On December 9, 2021, both the 
request and petition were rejected.
3. On December 30, 2021, Complainant J.Y. filed the instant 
constitutional complaint against Article 328, Section (1) and Articles 344 
and 361 of the Criminal Act.
D. 2024Hun-Ma146
1. Complainant C.M.-1 filed criminal complaints against her sibling 
C.M.-2 and his spouse J.J. for embezzling the funds held in the name of 
- 35 -the late J.S. (died on June 9, 2023), the mother of Complainant 
C.M.-1. On November 16, 2023, the chief of the Northern Chungcheong 
Province’s Eumseong County Police Station decided not to forward the 
case (absence of prosecutorial authority) to a prosecutor, citing that 
C.M.-2 and J.J. were a lineal descendant of the late J.S. and the spouse 
of C.M.-2, respectively, thereby qualifying for immunity (Case No. 
2023-003329). 
2. In response, Complainant C.M.-1 filed the instant constitutional 
complaint against Article 328, Section (1) of the Criminal Act on 
February 7, 2024.
II. Subject Matter of Review
The special provisions on punishment and prosecution for property 
crimes against relatives are referred to as the “immunity for crimes 
against relatives.” Article 328 of the Criminal Act sets out the basic 
content of this immunity, and Articles 344, 354, 361, and 365 of the 
Criminal Act apply Article 328 mutatis mutandis to individual property 
crimes. As a result, when the perpetrator and victim of a property crime 
share certain kinship relationships, either punishment is waived, or 
prosecution is allowed only upon complaint. Specifically, Article 328 
itself is the “immunity for crimes against relatives” provision applicable 
to the offense of interference with the exercise of rights under Article 
323 of the Criminal Act. However, since it is applied mutatis mutandis 
to most property crimes, Article 328 of the Criminal Act effectively 
functions as a general provision on the immunity for crimes against 
relatives.
If the Court strictly confines the clauses of the Criminal Act that 
provide for the immunity for crimes against relatives to the portions 
directly related to Complainants and applicable to the underlying cases, 
the subject matter of review should be narrowly defined as “the portions 
- 36 -of the provisions concerning the relevant relatives that apply Article 
328, Section (1) mutatis mutandis to the specific property crime in 
question.” However, considering that 1) the alleged infringement of 
fundamental rights or unconstitutionality asserted by Complainants lies in 
the uniform remission of punishment for certain property crimes between 
specific relatives, 2) the same standards of review apply to each 
individual provision that enacts the mutatis mutandis application of 
Article 328, and 3) the determination of the constitutionality of Article 
328 will impact all provisions where it is mutatis mutandis applied, 
separating the assessment by crime type or scope of relatives would be 
inappropriate from the perspectives of coherence in legal order and 
judicial economy. Therefore, here, the subject matter of review will 
directly be Article 328, Section (1) of the Criminal Act, rather than the 
individual mutatis mutandis provisions.
Accordingly, the subject matter of review in this case is whether 
Article 328, Section (1) of the Criminal Act (amended by Act No. 7427 
on March 31, 2005) (hereinafter referred to as the “Provision at Issue”) 
infringes the fundamental rights of Complainants K.J. and C.M.-1 
(2020Hun-Ma468, 2024Hun-Ma146) and whether it violates the 
Constitution (2020Hun-Ba341, 2021Hun-Ba420). The Provision at Issue 
and related provisions are as follows.
A. Provision at Issue
The current Criminal Act (amended by Act No. 7427 on March 31, 
2005)
Article 328 (Crimes and Complaints among Relatives)
(1)When the crime of Article 323 is committed against lineal blood 
relatives, spouses, relatives living together, family members living 
together, or their spouses, punishment therefor shall be remitted.
- 37 -B. Related Provisions
The current Criminal Act (amended by Act No. 7427 on March 31, 
2005)
Article 328 (Crimes and Complaints among Relatives) 
(2)When the crime of Article 323 is committed against relatives other 
than those in Section (1), the prosecution shall be instituted only upon 
complaint.
(3)The preceding Sections shall not apply to an accomplice who is 
not a relative within the meaning of the preceding two Sections.
The current Criminal Act (enacted by Act No. 293 on September 18, 
1953)
Article 354 (Crimes among Relatives, Energy)
The provisions of Articles 328 and 346 shall apply mutatis mutandi s 
to the crimes of this Chapter.
Article 361 (Crimes among Relatives, Energy)
The provisions of Articles 328 and 346 shall apply mutatis mutandis 
to the crimes of this Chapter.
The former Criminal Act (amended by Act No. 5057 on December 29, 
1995 and before amendment by Act No. 17571 on December 8, 2020)
Article 348 (Quasi-Fraud)
(1)A person who, by taking advantage of the lack of critical faculty 
of a minor or the mental disorders of another, takes property or obtains 
pecuniary advantage from the latter, shall be punished by imprisonment 
with labor for not more than ten years or by a fine not exceeding 20 
million won.
- 38 -The current Criminal Act (amended by Act No. 5057 on December 
29, 1995)
Article 355 (Embezzlement and Breach of Trust)
(1)A person who, having the custody of another’s property, embezzles 
or refuses to return it, shall be punished by imprisonment with labor for 
not more than five years or by a fine not exceeding 15 million won.
Article 356 (Occupational Embezzlement, Occupational Breach of Trust)
A person who commits the crime as prescribed in Article 355 in 
violation of the duties of one’s occupation, shall be punished by 
imprisonment with labor for not more than ten years or by a fine not 
exceeding 30 million won.
The current Criminal Procedure Act (enacted by Act No. 341 on 
September 23, 1954)
Article 322 (Remission of Penalty and Judgment of Suspension of 
Pronouncement of Punishment)
Where a remission of penalty or suspension of pronouncement of 
punishment is to be rendered in a case pending against the defendant, 
such remission or suspension shall be made by judgment.
III. Arguments of Complainants
A. 2020Hun-Ma468
1.The Provision at Issue violates the State’s duty to protect its 
citizens from crime by failing to take any measures to protect victims. 
Consequently, exploitation of labor, income, and social security benefits 
by family members has been frequent, and sham marriages to exploit the 
system are also emerging.
- 39 -2.The Provision at Issue infringes the victim’s right to make a 
statement during trial, the right to the pursuit of happiness, the right to 
equality, and the right to property by violating the rule against excessive 
restriction. Given the current changes in the structure and culture of 
families and relatives, this provision has lost the legitimacy of its 
legislative purpose. The proper exercise of the State’s penal power is 
necessary to prevent crimes within families and, moreover, to maintain 
the family system. Furthermore, since there are less restrictive means 
available through the regulations on crimes subject to victims’ 
complaints or offenses that cannot be prosecuted with the victim’s 
express objections, and given that the scope of relatives exempted from 
punishment is excessively broad, this provision meets neither the least 
restrictive means test nor the balance of interests test. Additionally, no 
rational basis exists for distinguishing between the relatives specified in 
the above provisions and other relatives.
3.The Provision at Issue remits punishment for property crimes 
against relatives based solely on the relationship between the victim and 
the offender, uniformly disregarding the varying degrees of unlawfulness 
based on factors such as the severity of harm, the nature of the crime, 
or the types of the criminal act. This approach is inconsistent with the 
legal framework of the Criminal Act and the Criminal Procedure Act 
and undermines citizens’ predictability and trust regarding crime and 
punishment.
B. 2020Hun-Ba341
The Provision at Issue expands the scope of remission to include cases 
where the offender is an in-law, infringing the victim’s right to the 
pursuit of happiness, right to equality, right to property, and freedom of 
family life.
- 40 -C. 2021Hun-Ba420
The Provision at Issue was enacted by adopting the Roman law 
tradition of “the law does not cross the threshold of the home.” It 
uncritically accepted the Japanese Criminal Act, conflicting with our 
traditional legal consciousness. This provision, which induces heinous 
property crimes against relatives, is incompatible with our legal tradition 
and does not align with recent public opinion.
D. 2024Hun-Ma146
1. The Provision at Issue functions as a waiver of accountability for 
malicious property crimes committed against vulnerable individuals, such 
as the elderly or disabled, by those in a familial relationship, and thus 
lacks legitimacy. Even if there is a need to restrain State intervention in 
property crimes against relatives, there are various methods available, 
such as allowing the complainant to withdraw a criminal complaint or 
express an objection to punishment, depending on the remorse shown or 
restitution made after investigation. Despite these alternatives, the 
Provision at Issue wholly excludes the State’s penal power in property 
crimes against relatives, thereby failing to satisfy the least restrictive 
means test. Also, it unduly restricts the victim’s right to make a 
statement during trial across a wide range of crimes, thus failing to 
satisfy the balance of interests test. Consequently, this provision infringes 
the right to make a statement during trial by going against the rule 
against excessive restriction.
2. The Provision at Issue discriminates between victims of property 
crimes committed by relatives and victims of other property crimes and 
thus infringes the right to equality.
- 41 -IV. Assessment
A. Applicable Scope and Legal Nature of Immunity for Crimes against 
Relatives
1. Applicable Crimes
The Criminal Act provides in Article 328 that, for the offense of 
interference with the exercise of rights (Article 323), if the offender and 
the victim share a specified kinship, punishment shall be remitted, or the 
prosecution shall be instituted only upon complaint. The Criminal Act 
applies this provision mutatis mutandis to all other property crimes 
except for robbery and criminal damage. Specifically, Article 344 of the 
Criminal Act applies this leniency to larceny (Article 329), compound 
larceny at night (Article 330), special larceny (Article 331), unlawful use 
of automobile (Article 331-2), and habitual crimes (Article 332) and 
attempts of each offense. Article 354 applies this provision to fraud 
(Article 347), fraud by use of computer, etc. (Article 347-2), quasi-fraud 
(Article 348), unlawful use of facilities for convenience (Article 348-2), 
unjustifiable profit (Article 349), extortion (Article 350), special extortion 
(Article 350-2), and habitual crimes and attempts of each offense 
(Articles 351, 352). Article 361 applies the same leniency to embezzlement 
and breach of trust (Article 355), occupational embezzlement and breach 
of trust (Article 356), receiving or giving bribe by breach of trust 
(Article 357), attempts of each offense (Article 359), and embezzlements 
of lost articles (Article 360). For offenses involving stolen property, if 
the offender and the victim share a relationship as specified in Article 
328, Section (1) or (2), Article 328 is applied mutatis mutandis 
(Criminal Act, Article 365, Section (1)).
Additionally, immunity for crimes against relatives applies even in 
cases where a special law imposes aggravated punishment for the above 
property crimes unless there is an express provision excluding such 
application. 
- 42 -2. Scope of Applicable Relatives
(a) The Civil Act defines the scope of relatives to whom the rule of 
immunity for crimes against relatives is applied. Relatives include 
spouses, blood relatives, and relatives by affinity (Civil Act, Article 
767). Blood relatives encompass lineal blood relatives (lineal ascendants 
and descendants) and collateral blood relatives (siblings, siblings’ lineal 
descendants, lineal ascendants’ siblings, and those siblings’ lineal 
descendants) (Civil Act, Article 768). Relatives by affinity refer to the 
spouses of blood relatives, the blood relatives of a spouse, and the 
spouses of those blood relatives (Civil Act, Article 769). The legal effect 
of kinship extends to blood relatives within the eighth degree of 
relationship, relatives by affinity within the fourth degree of relationship, 
and spouses (Civil Act, Article 777).
(b) Under Article 328, Section (1) of the Criminal Act, the relatives 
eligible for remission of punishment include “lineal blood relatives, 
spouses, relatives living together, family members living together, or 
their spouses.” The term “lineal blood relatives” refers to lineal 
ascendants and descendants. “Spouse” is limited to a de jure spouse, 
excluding de facto relationships or extramarital affairs, regardless of 
cohabitation. “Relatives living together” (cohabiting relatives) denotes 
relatives who share both a residence and daily life. “Family members 
living together” (cohabiting family members) encompasses the relatives 
listed in Article 779 of the Civil Act who are also “relatives living 
together,” namely spouses, lineal blood relatives, siblings, and those who 
share a livelihood, i.e., the spouses of lineal blood relatives, the lineal 
blood relatives of spouses, and the siblings of spouses. Additionally, 
“their spouses,” as specified in the Provision at Issue, includes not only 
the spouses of cohabiting family members but also the spouses of lineal 
blood relatives and cohabiting relatives (Supreme Court Judgment 
2011Do1765, May 13, 2011).
- 43 -Article 328, Section (2) of the Criminal Act provides that, for property 
crimes against relatives not covered by the above definition, prosecution 
may only proceed upon complaint.
3. Legal Nature of Immunity for Crimes against Relatives
Article 328, Section (1) of the Criminal Act prescribes a ground for 
remission of punishment, while Article 328, Section (2) establishes a 
criminal complaint as a precondition to prosecution.
B. Summary of Issues
1. Article 27, Section (5) of the Constitution provides that “A criminal 
victim shall be entitled to make a statement during the proceedings of 
the trial of the case involved, as prescribed by Act.” This provision 
guarantees criminal victims the right to make a statement during trial. 
Under the current prosecutorial monopoly system, which excludes private 
prosecution by victims and grants exclusive prosecutorial authority to 
public prosecutors, this right aims to ensure procedural propriety in 
criminal justice by allowing criminal victims the opportunity to express 
their opinions beyond merely testifying as witnesses in the trial of their 
case (see Constitutional Court 2002Hun-Ma533, September 25, 2003; 
2010Hun-Ma243, October 25, 2011). Additionally, the right to make a 
statement during trial not only requires judges to consider the victim’s 
statements in delivering fair and equitable judgments but also actively 
guarantees criminal victims a fundamental judicial procedural right 
to request that the judge exercise appropriate penal authority (see 
Constitutional Court 88Hun-Ma3, April 17, 1989).
Since the Provision at Issue mandates that the court issue a judgment 
remitting punishment regardless of, inter alia, the criminal victim’s 
wishes, it restricts the criminal victim’s right to make a statement during 
trial. 
- 44 -However, statutory reservation regarding this right, prescribed by 
Article 27, Section (5) of the Constitution, corresponds to the so-called 
“formative statutory reservation of fundamental rights,” and the 
legislature has freedom in shaping laws to determine the specific content 
of this constitutional guarantee; therefore, a constitutional violation would 
arise only if such legislative discretion were manifestly unreasonable, 
exceeding the bounds of discretion (see Constitutional Court 2002 
Hun-Ma533, September 25, 2003; 2010Hun-Ma243, October 25, 2011).
Thus, the Court will examine whether the Provision at Issue is 
manifestly unreasonable, infringing the criminal victim’s right to make a 
statement during trial.
2. Complainants argue that the Provision at Issue infringes the right to 
equality by treating property crimes against relatives differently from 
other property crimes and by distinguishing victims of property crimes 
committed by certain relatives from victims of other types of crimes 
committed by those relatives. However, this claim essentially contends 
that the rule of immunity for relatives infringes the judicial procedural 
right of criminal victims to request the court to exercise appropriate 
penal authority. Since the Court is evaluating whether the criminal 
victim’s right to make a statement during trial has been infringed, a 
separate analysis of the above claim is unnecessary.
Complainants further claim an infringement of equality rights by 
comparing the application of the Provision at Issue to that of Article 
328, Section (2) of the Criminal Act. However, the Provision at Issue 
pertains to a ground for remission of punishment, while Article 328, 
Section (2) concerns preconditions to prosecution; thus, they are not 
directly comparable.
3. A legal system granting exclusive prosecutorial power to State 
organs, disallowing victims from seeking revenge, and allowing self-help 
only in very limited circumstances functions effectively only if the State 
- 45 -adequately fulfills its duty to protect victims. Therefore, if the State 
fails to protect citizens from crime, such failure may constitute an 
infringement of citizens’ fundamental rights (see Constitutional Court 
88Hun-Ma3, April 17, 1989). However, Complainants’ claim regarding 
the “State’s failure to fulfill its protective duty” is substantively the same 
as the claim that the criminal victim’s right to make a statement during 
trial has been infringed, so it will not be examined separately.
4.Complainants argue that the Provision at Issue infringes their 
property rights, freedom of family life, and right to the pursuit of 
happiness. However, as the Provision at Issue concerns the State’s 
exercise of penal authority over perpetrators of criminal acts, it does not 
directly limit these fundamental rights. Therefore, no further analysis is 
warranted.
C.Infringement of Criminal Victims’ Right to Make Statements during 
Trial
1. The intent behind the rule of immunity for crimes against relatives 
is to maintain family peace by preventing its disruption through criminal 
punishment, and this rule reflects a policy consideration that the State’s 
penal intervention in internal family matters should be avoided (see 
Constitutional Court 2010Hun-Ba89, March 29, 2012).
The family is considered the fundamental unit of society and a 
community where members autonomously, continuously, and comprehensively 
protect and support one another, characterized by altruistic bonds, mutual 
trust, and cooperation. Similarly, kinship relationships that extend from the 
family exhibit similar traits. When property crimes are committed within 
this contex t—excluding those involving serious harm to life, physical 
safety, or freedom —it is often efficient to allow the family or kinship 
network to autonomously restore any harm and reach reconciliation and 
forgiveness. This approach aligns with the interests of all family members, 
- 46 -including the victim, particularly when the harm involves legally 
protected interests that are subject to free disposal and can generally be 
redressed with relative ease. Furthermore, in many cases, given the 
longstanding psychological and economic dependence, trust, expectations, 
and emotional ties that family members develop, penal intervention may 
not be necessary.
Thus, considering the historical and cultural features of family and 
kinship relationships in our country, the nature of property crimes, and 
the supplementary role of criminal punishment, a special provision for 
prosecuting or penalizing tolerable levels of property crimes occurring 
among close family members sharing economic interests or emotional 
bonds is understandable.
2. The Provision at Issue mandates a uniform remission of punishment 
when certain kinship relationships exist between the perpetrator of a 
property crime and the victim, without assessing factors such as the 
actual bond between them, the victim’s intent to punish, the nature of 
the offense, or the extent of harm caused. This forces judges to issue a 
mandatory remission of punishment based solely on the existence of 
particular kinship relationships, without due consideration of these 
specifics.
This approach could lead to outcomes that are misaligned with the 
system’s purpose, particularly given the excessively broad scope of 
relatives covered. Historically, under the extended family structure of an 
agrarian society, property was commonly viewed as a shared resource 
among blood-related family groups, and property acquisition or 
expenditure typically occurred within a shared living environment. In 
such cases, it may have been reasonable to restore harm autonomously, 
and foster reconciliation and forgiveness within the family unit.
However, in our society today, there is a strong trend toward smaller 
family sizes and simpler family structures, with nuclear families 
becoming more common and single-person households rapidly increasing. 
- 47 -The industrial structure has also evolved, with agriculture, forestry, and 
fisheries now occupying a smaller share, while manufacturing and 
service industries have seen significant growth, leading to economic 
activities that differ markedly from those of the past. Consequently, it 
has become difficult to assume shared economic interests or the ease of 
redress and reconciliation within kinship groups. Kinship bonds and trust 
are no longer absolute; rather, they are shaped by shifts in social culture, 
industrial structure, and modes of lifestyle including patterns of economic 
activity among people of the era. 
When examining the specific relatives to whom the blanket remission 
of punishment under the Provision at Issue applies, the Court finds that 
“lineal blood relatives” include relationships such as lineal ascendants 
(e.g., parents, grandparents, great-grandparents) and lineal descendants 
(e.g., children, grandchildren). These are close, direct blood relations, 
where emotional bonds and attachments typically develop from the 
descendant’s birth, which often makes it reasonable to apply “immunity 
for crimes against relatives” provisions. However, this may not always 
be the case. In the case of a spouse, it is difficult to make a uniform 
judgment on intimacy, emotional bonds, or shared economic interests, 
especially as factors like the nature of the marriage or cohabitation status 
are not considered. For cohabiting family members or relatives, while the 
concept of kinship follows the Civil Act and is limited by the condition 
of “cohabitation,” considering that the legal effects of kinship apply to 
“blood relatives within the eighth degree of relationship, relatives by 
affinity within the fourth degree of relationship, and spouses,” the scope 
remains broad, making it extremely difficult to generalize the nature of 
these relationships. Moreover, for spouses of lineal blood relatives, 
cohabiting relatives, and cohabiting family members, the earlier difficulty 
in generalizing kinship relationships becomes more pronounced.
Uniform remission of punishment across a broad range of kinship 
relationships, without considering these nuances, sacrifices the rights of 
criminally victimized family members in favor of upholding the mere 
- 48 -formality of family and kinship structures, potentially conflicting with 
the intended purpose of the rule.
3.The Provision at Issue applies mutatis mutandis to all property 
crimes except robbery and criminal damage. Yet, it is difficult to argue 
that the unlawfulness of such property crimes is generally minor enough 
to be tolerable to the victim or that restitution and restoration of kinship 
relationships are easily achievable.
In cases like quasi-fraud, embezzlement, and occupational embezzlement 
—all of which Complainants claim to have suffere d—quasi-fraud carries 
a statutory penalty of “imprisonment with labor for not more than ten years 
or a fine not exceeding 20 million won”; embezzlement, “imprisonment 
with labor for not more than five years or a fine not exceeding 15 million 
won”; and occupational embezzlement, “imprisonment with labor for not 
more than ten years or a fine not exceeding 30 million won.” These 
offenses may therefore be regarded as severely unlawful depending on the 
nature of the crime. Further, under the “Act on the Aggravated Punishment, 
etc. of Specific Economic Crimes,” embezzlement or occupationa l 
embezzlement involving a gain of 500 million to less than 5 billion won 
can result in “imprisonment with labor for a limited term or not less than 
three years” and a gain of 5 billion won or more can lead to “imprisonment 
with labor for life or not less than five years,” underscoring the gravity 
of these crimes.
Additionally, several property crimes, which include physical violence 
or threats (extortion) that limit the victim’s free will, or the carrying of 
a weapon or the joint action by two or more individuals (special 
larceny), are not crimes where recovery of damages or restoration of 
relationships can be easily assumed taking into account constituent 
elements of the crimes.
4. The assumption that property crimes among certain relatives can 
and should be resolved autonomously within the family or kinship 
- 49 -society is valid only when the victim is capable of making 
independent and voluntary decisions. However, when the victim is 
dependent on other members within the family or kinship society and 
unable to fully grasp the significance of economic or transactional 
decisions —such as in cases where they are a minor or lack capacity to 
manage affairs due to mental constraints from illness, disability, old age, 
or other factors — applying or extending the Provision at Issue could 
result in economic exploitation of vulnerable members within the family 
and kinship society. Article 88-3 of the Act on Welfare of Persons with 
Disabilities, as amended by Act No. 18333 on July 27, 2021 (effective 
January 28, 2022), partially addresses this issue by excluding certain 
property crimes related to fraud, extortion, embezzlement, and breach of 
trust, from immunity when the crimes constitute abuse of persons with 
disabilities. However, the scope of these exceptions is limited and may 
not be sufficient to fully protect crime victims.
5. Nevertheless, the Provision at Issue imposes an inflexible rule 
requiring the judge to issue a judgment remitting punishment, without 
taking such factors into account. 
A judgment remitting punishment legally constitutes a substantive 
conviction. However, in prosecutorial practice, if an individual is 
remitted from punishment by the application or mutatis mutandis 
application of the Provision at Issue, a decision of non-prosecution due 
to lack of prosecutorial authority is issued under Article 115, Section (3), 
Item 4, Sub-item (g) of the Prosecutorial Case Management Rule, which 
mandates a decision of non-prosecution due to lack of prosecutorial 
authority when punishment is remitted by law. Consequently, in most 
cases, prosecution is not instituted, resulting in the criminal victim 
entirely losing any opportunity to participate in the trial process. In rare 
instances where prosecution is instituted due to uncertainty over the 
application or mutatis mutandis application of the Provision at Issue 
during the investigative stage, any request by the criminal victim for the 
- 50 -court to exercise appropriate penal authority holds limited practical 
significance in proceedings where the outcome is a predetermined 
“remission of punishment.”
6. Examining the legislation of civil law countries that also adopted 
“immunity for crimes against relatives” provisions according to Roman 
law traditions, the Court finds that Germany, for instance, classifies 
certain property crimes such as theft, embezzlement, and fraud as crimes 
prosecutable only upon complaint if the victim is a relative, the guardian 
or the carer of the offender or if the victim lives in the same household 
as the offender. France similarly limits prosecution for property crimes, 
such as theft, coercion, and fraud, when the perpetrator is a lineal blood 
relative or cohabiting spouse, while excluding, inter alia, cases involving 
items essential for daily life or where the perpetrator is a guardian. 
Austria also defines property crimes such as criminal damage and theft 
committed by spouses, registered domestic partners, lineal blood 
relatives, siblings, or cohabiting relatives, as prosecutable only upon 
complaint by the victim and also provides for mitigation of punishment. 
Thus, there are few instances where a mandatory remission of 
punishment or the prohibition of prosecution regardless of a criminal 
complaint is uniformly applied for property crimes committed by a broad 
range of relatives. It should be noted that, even in such instances, the 
scope of relatives and property crimes is much narrower than what is 
provided under our Criminal Act.
7. Considering the above factors together, the Court finds that the 
Provision at Issue prevents criminal victims from requesting the judge to 
exercise appropriate penal authority. This constitutes a clear deviation 
from legislative discretion, rendering it patently unreasonable or unfair, 
and thus infringes the criminal victim’s right to make a statement during 
trial.
- 51 -D.Need for Decision of Nonconformity to Constitution with Suspended 
Application
The Provision at Issue infringes the right of a criminal victim to make 
a statement during trial. Therefore, as a rule, a decision of simple 
unconstitutionality should be rendered for the Provision at Issue. 
However, the unconstitutionality of the Provision at Issue does not arise 
from recognizing special treatment for certain property crimes among 
specific relatives. Rather, it stems from the blanket remission of 
punishment, applied uniformly to a broad range of relatives regardless of 
the severity of the property crime or the victim’s will. This approach 
can, in specific cases, render the criminal victim’s right to make a 
statement during trial ineffective.
To eliminate the unconstitutionality of the Provision at Issue, several 
options could be considered, such as making the victim’s expression of 
intent to punish the offender a precondition for prosecution. This 
approach would take into account the victim’s assessment of practical 
family or kinship relationships, the extent of harm, and the potential for 
restoring trust and bonds among family members or relatives. The 
legislature should explore such options with sufficient social consensus.
Therefore, instead of rendering a decision of simple unconstitutionality, 
the Court issues a decision of nonconformity to the Constitution for the 
Provision at Issue but suspends its application. The legislature is 
obligated to amend the Provision at Issue as soon as possible, and no 
later than December 31, 2025. If an amendment is not made by that 
date, the Provision at Issue will become null and void as of January 1, 
2026.
V. Conclusion 
As the Provision at Issue is inconsistent with the Constitution, the 
Court issues a decision of nonconformity to the Constitution, ordering 
- 52 -suspension of its application until amended by the legislature no later 
than December 31, 2025. This decision was made with a unanimous 
opinion of the participating Justices, as set forth in Holding.
Justices Lee Jongseok (Presiding Justice), Lee Eunae, Lee Youngjin, 
Kim Kiyoung, Moon Hyungbae, Lee Mison, Kim Hyungdu, Jung 
Jungmi, and Cheong Hyungsik
- 53 -Appendix
List of Complainants
(Omitted )
